output:
(B).

The model predicts *who will churn*, not *who can be saved*. Those are different questions. A customer flagged at 95% accuracy may have already mentally left — the discount arrives too late to change anything. High predictive accuracy says nothing about whether the predicted group is *persuadable*.

The core error is treating a predictive model as if it were a causal one. To drive retention, you need a model that identifies customers whose behavior *changes in response to intervention* — an uplift/causal model — not one that simply spots the most obvious churn signals.
